profile.name: Michael
profile.role_in_household: Primary client; employed executive and the one who tends to bring the statements and lead on investment/account details.
profile.financial_literacy_level: Moderate-to-high: comfortable discussing account types, balances, and retirement timing, but may mix up insurance terms (premium vs. benefit) and occasionally needs help translating goals into a plan.
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Conservative in practice: prefers protecting principal, gets uneasy with big swings, and wants clear downside explanations before agreeing to changes; tends to favor “steady and predictable” over “maximize returns.”
profile.communication_style: Direct and somewhat fast-paced; uses round numbers first and then checks paperwork for exact figures. Will say things like “I think it’s around…” and then correct himself once he sees the statement. Can interrupt briefly to clarify a number.
emotional_tendencies: relieved by clear structure and checklists
profile.decision_making_style: Decides after seeing a concise comparison (before/after, pros/cons) and understanding the impact on monthly cash flow; prefers incremental changes rather than sweeping moves.
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Cautiously open: willing to engage, but trust increases only after the advisor demonstrates accuracy, follow-through, and sensitivity around prior-marriage obligations.
internal_biases: mental accounting (separates “house money,” retirement, and obligations)
hidden_concerns: worries that alimony and the mortgage could constrain retirement options
concerned about health and insurability without wanting to dwell on it

profile.name: Mary
profile.role_in_household: Spouse/partner; employed business owner who focuses on big-picture goals, retirement lifestyle, and keeping plans organized, while letting Michael handle most account-by-account specifics.
profile.financial_literacy_level: Moderate: understands budgeting and planning concepts and asks good clarifying questions, but tends not to recall exact balances and may conflate gross vs. net or payment vs. balance unless prompted.
profile.risk_attitude_behavioral: Cautious but goal-oriented: generally aligned with conservative choices, yet will support measured risk if it clearly improves retirement readiness; prefers guardrails (limits, buffers, and clear contingencies).
profile.communication_style: Warm, collaborative, and reflective; speaks in ranges and priorities (“what matters most is…”). Uses hedging like “maybe” and “I’m not totally sure,” and often asks the advisor to restate recommendations in plain language.
emotional_tendencies: sensitive to fairness and family dynamics
profile.decision_making_style: Consensus-driven: wants to talk through tradeoffs together, then sleep on major decisions; values a written summary and next steps.
profile.trust_level_toward_advisor: Moderate: tends to extend initial trust if the advisor listens well and respects boundaries, but will disengage if the conversation feels overly salesy or dismissive.
internal_biases: anchoring to familiar monthly budget numbers
planning fallacy (optimistic about how smoothly transitions will go)
hidden_concerns: wants reassurance that estate planning and beneficiary choices won’t create tension in a second marriage
concerned about retirement timing and maintaining independence without overburdening Michael
